policy_statement: Set intermediate building-stock targets: city/CPAS public buildings to reach average EPC C+; SISP social housing renovation plan to meet regional average EPC C+ by 2040 with priorities met by 2030; CPAS/Régie homes rated EPC F/G to be downgraded by 2033.
mechanism_description: The city sets intermediate EPC improvement targets and a renovation plan for public buildings and social housing, leading to building upgrades that reduce overall building energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (heated/cooled/used floor area) → ↓ energy input → ↓ fuel/electricity demand → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public building and social-housing owners/operators must meet interim EPC/energy targets and follow renovation plans/guidelines, prompting upgrades such as insulation/airtightness, window replacement, efficient heating/cooling and ventilation, heat recovery, controls, and system optimization (sometimes replacement/demolition decisions where relevant).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved building envelope and systems reduce heat losses/gains and improve conversion/control efficiency, lowering delivered energy needed to provide the same indoor comfort and building services.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower site energy use reduces upstream and on-site combustion emissions (and can also reduce peak demand), yielding net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption, which in turn reduces GHG emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily acts by reducing the energy input required to deliver the same building services through retrofits driven by EPC/renovation targets.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the ultimate benefit is mitigation (lower GHGs), the direct and defining lever across all such policies is improved energy efficiency/energy-use reduction—i.e., resource/energy efficiency—rather than hazard reduction (adaptation) or ecosystem change (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific program’s dominant change were fuel switching to renewables (e.g., district heat decarbonization) rather than energy-use reduction, that would be a different canonical mechanism; here, the stated mechanism is explicitly “energy_use_reduction.” Demolition/new-build considerations remain within the mechanism only insofar as they serve lowering operational energy demand; embodied-carbon impacts are not the dominant pathway described.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy sharing this mechanism—retrofit targets/renovation plans intended to reduce building energy consumption—fits Resource Efficiency as the primary category because the central causal step is reduced energy input.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any more specific subtype (e.g., envelope vs HVAC vs controls; public vs social housing; inclusion of renewables) requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations bundle renewables (PV/heat pumps) with retrofits; if emissions cuts are driven mainly by supply decarbonization rather than lower demand, primary classification could shift toward Mitigation for that specific policy. Embodied emissions from renovations/demolition can be material but are not the dominant pathway in the described mechanism.
key_indicators: "three intermediate targets have been set"; "will achieve an average Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) of C+"; "there will be a renovation plan"; "to meet the regional objective"; "most energy-intensive homes (EPC F and G)"; "by 2030"; "by 2040"; "by 2033"; "energy neutrality for public buildings by 2040"
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort/health; reduced energy bills/energy poverty (social housing); reduced local air pollutants from heating fuels; improved asset condition/value
instance_edge_case_notes: “downgraded” for EPC F/G homes is ambiguous (could mean upgraded/renovated or removed from the stock); the excerpt indicates targets/plan but does not specify enforcement mechanism, financing, or specific retrofit standards.
